result:
Two main strategies.

Chaining: each bucket holds linked list (or tree). Collisions append to list. Simple, degrades to O(n) if many collide, but tolerates high load factor.

Open addressing: on collision, probe for another slot in same array. Variants:
- linear probing: check next slot, then next. Cache-friendly but clusters.
- quadratic probing: step by 1,4,9... reduces clustering.
- double hashing: second hash decides step size. Best spread, worst cache.

Open addressing needs resize before table fills (~0.7 load factor). Deletion needs tombstones so probes don't stop early.

Real implementations mix tricks. Java HashMap: chaining, but converts bucket to red-black tree past 8 entries. Python dict, Go map, Rust HashMap: open addressing with their own probe schemes. Robin Hood hashing evens out probe distances by swapping entries during insert.

Good hash function matters more than collision strategy — if hash distributes poorly, neither saves you.
